a  This paper  presents  a novel  fall  detection system that
uses computer vision, specifically pose estimation,  to detect
falls through a fixed camera. Unlike traditional sensor-based
methods,  our  approach eliminates  the need for  additional
hardware or wearables, providing a more affordable and non-
intrusive solution for fall detection in elderly care settings.
By analyzing key body landmarks and movement patterns, the
system can distinguish between normal activities and falls with
high accuracy.
The primary contributions of this work are twofold: first,
we leverage the lightweight MediaPipe framework to perform
real-time pose estimation on standard CPUs, making  the
system both computationally efficient and accessible; second,
we introduce a voting mechanism combined with a 20-frame

Fall detection has been widely studied using various ap-
proaches,  including wearable  sensors,  video-based motion
tracking, and machine learning techniques. Wearable sensor
systems  such as accelerometers,  gyroscopes,  and pressure
sensors are commonly used to detect falls based on movement
or impact. While these systems are generally accurate,  they
require users  to wear devices consistently, which can be
problematic for elderly individuals who may forget or refuse
to wear them regularly.
In contrast,  vision-based systems offer  a more passive
solution by using cameras  to track human motion. These
systems can use techniques like Convolutional Neural Net-
works  (CNNs)  and optical flow to detect  falls based on
body movement and pose. However, CNN-based models are
often computationally intensive, requiring powerful hardware
such as GPUs, which makes them impractical for real-time
applications in resource-constrained environments like elderly
care homes. Additionally,  such systems can be sensitive to
changes  in lighting conditions, occlusion, or clutter  in the
environment, which affects their robustness.
More recently, there has been a shift toward pose estimation
frameworks, which aim to capture human body landmarks
from video  input. These  frameworks,  such as MediaPipe,
offer a lightweight alternative to CNNs by detecting body
joints and key points in real-time, using only standard CPUs
with minimal computational overhead. MediaPipe has shown
promise in various applications, including fall detection, due
to its efficiency and accuracy in estimating human pose under
different conditions.
Several studies have combined pose estimation with multi-
frame buffering  techniques  to  improve detection accuracy
across time. This approach helps retain critical motion fea-
tures over multiple frames,  reducing false positives caused
by temporary changes in posture. Moreover, recent work has
incorporated weighted voting mechanisms, where different
pose-based features are assigned varying levels of importance
based on their relevance to fall detection. This enables more

B. Reducing False Alarms
Elderly care homes often experience a high volume of
routine activities such as sitting, bending, or lying down, all
of which could be misinterpreted as falls by conventional no
isfall Update Buffer
fall detection systems. To address this challenge, our system
integrates a 20-frame buffer memory and a weighted voting
mechanism. The buffer helps maintain the impact of posture yes
changes over 20 frames, ensuring that only sustained, fall-like
movements are considered. In addition, the weighted voting
Alert
system assigns higher importance to key features (such as
height ratio and torso-leg angle) that are most indicative of
a fall. This reduces false positives, minimizing unnecessary Fig.1. FlowchartoftheFallDetectionSystem.
alerts and ensuring that caregivers are notified only when
genuine fall events occur, allowing them to focus on real
emergencies.

4:  for each indicator do

To evaluate the performance of the proposed fall detection
system, we conducted experiments using the ”GMDCSA24:
A Dataset  for Human Fall Detection in Videos” [1]. The
system’s performance was evaluated based on several key
metrics, including accuracy, precision, and recall. The results
are summarized in Table II.
A. Dataset and Setup
The fall detection system was tested on the GMDCSA-24
dataset, which contains over 130 video clips recorded in a
controlled environment. The dataset includes fall and non-fall
activities performed by four subjects. Each video in the dataset
is in RGB format, with a total size of 1.95 GB. The activities
in the dataset consist of various movements, such as sitting,
standing, walking, bending, and falling, commonly observed
in elderly care facilities.
Fig. 2.  Pose Detection
Fig. 3.  Fall Detected

The fall detection system demonstrates robust performance
across multiple evaluation metrics. The model achieves high
recall  (98.68%)  indicating excellent capability in detecting
actual falls with minimal false negatives, while maintaining
strong precision (88.24%). The overall accuracy of 91.54%
and F1-Score of 93.17% demonstrate the system’s balanced
and reliable performance in real-world fall detection scenarios.

Recall (Sensitivity)  98.68%  Ability to detect actual falls
Specificity  81.48%  Ability to correctly identify non-falls
F1-Score  93.17%  Harmonic mean of precision & recall
A. Results and Discussion
The system demonstrated high recall, which indicates its
strong ability to correctly identify fall events. The precision
value shows  that  the system successfully minimizes  false
positives, although there is some room for  improvement  in
reducing false alarms. The accuracy of 86.86% is considered
satisfactory for real-time fall detection systems, as it strikes a
balance between correctly identifying falls and avoiding false
positives in a dynamic environment.
One of  the main strengths of  the system is  its ability
to detect  falls in real-time without  requiring any additional
hardware or specialized sensors. The use of MediaPipe for
pose estimation ensures that the system can be implemented
on devices with minimal  computational  resources, making
it highly suitable for elderly care environments where cost-
effective solutions are essential.
B. Comparison with Existing Systems
Compared to traditional sensor-based fall detection systems,
our approach does not require the user to wear any devices
or rely on expensive sensor setups. Additionally, video-based
fall detection methods that use convolutional neural networks
(CNNs) often require substantial computational resources and
specialized hardware.  In contrast,  our  approach  leverages
lightweight pose estimation models such as MediaPipe, en-
abling real-time performance with a fixed camera setup.
Previous fall detection systems often rely on basic motion
detection or sensor readings, which may be limited in their
ability to differentiate between fall and non-fall activities, es-
pecially in dynamic environments like elderly care homes. Our
approach, which incorporates temporal analysis through the

20-frame buffer and voting mechanism, significantly reduces
false positives and ensures accurate detection in various real-
world scenarios.

In this paper, we presented a lightweight and efficient fall
detection system aimed at  improving safety in elderly care
facilities. The system leverages the MediaPipe pose estima-
tion framework to process video feeds in real-time, without
requiring any additional sensors or expensive hardware. By
analyzing key body landmarks and utilizing a 20-frame buffer
with a simple voting mechanism, the system accurately detects
falls while minimizing false positives.
The evaluation results show that the system achieves an ac-
curacy of 86.86%, precision of 85.23%, and recall of 93.75%.
These results highlight the system’s ability to effectively detect
falls while reducing unnecessary alerts, making it a reliable
solution for elderly care environments where prompt interven-
tion is critical. Additionally,  the system’s low computational
requirements make it well-suited for deployment on standard
CPUs, ensuring that it is both cost-effective and accessible.
Our approach demonstrates a significant improvement over
traditional  sensor-based and CNN-based fall detection sys-
tems, offering a practical, unobtrusive solution for continuous
monitoring in assisted living homes.

While the current system shows promising results, there are
several avenues for improvement. One key area is enhancing
the voting mechanism. Instead of relying on a simple voting
system, a weighted voting model could be explored. In this
approach, features like height ratio, torso-leg angle, and head-
floor distance could be assigned different weights based on
their  relevance and impact on fall detection accuracy. For
example, the torso-leg angle may carry more weight than knee-
ankle position,  as  the former  is more indicative of a fall.
By integrating weighted voting, we can prioritize the most
correlated features, leading to a more robust and reliable fall
detection system. Additionally, exploring the use of machine
learning models to dynamically adjust these weights based on
training data could further improve performance.
